meta_title: Window Screen Cleaning Guide for Shuswap Homeowners
meta_desc: When to clean window screens, DIY vs professional, and why cottonwood pollen and lake humidity make screens a bigger issue in Salmon Arm than most regions.
h1: Window Screen Cleaning Guide: What Shuswap Homeowners Need to Know
intro: Window screens do their job invisibly when they're clean—and visibly when they're not. A dirty screen reduces airflow, blocks natural light, retains moisture that feeds mould, and passes a fine layer of pollen and particulate into your home every time a breeze moves through. In the Shuswap, where cottonwood season and lake humidity create aggressive screen-fouling conditions, screen cleaning deserves more attention than most homeowners give it.

sections.h2: When Do Window Screens Actually Need Cleaning?
sections.body:
Most homeowners clean their screens when they visually appear dirty—when they can see accumulated debris. That's a reasonable baseline, but waiting for visible fouling means the screen has already progressed past the point of affecting air quality and airflow.

Several conditions indicate that cleaning is due regardless of visual appearance. If allergy symptoms worsen when windows are open versus closed, screen contamination is a likely contributor—even a fine, visually subtle coating of pollen on screen mesh significantly increases the pollen load carried indoors by breezes. If you notice reduced airflow through open windows compared to when screens are removed, the screen mesh is clogged enough to restrict air movement.

Visual indicators worth acting on: any visible pollen coating (yellow or grey-green haze on the mesh), visible mildew darkening at frame corners, cottonwood fibres embedded in the mesh that persist after light brushing, or any fuzzy growth on the aluminum frame. Post-winter appearance should always be assessed—screens that spent winter in place typically carry moisture-driven growth that isn't obvious until you look closely.

For Shuswap-area homes, the practical answer is twice-yearly cleaning as a baseline: once in late spring after cottonwood season, once in fall before screens are removed or covered for winter. Lakefront properties and homes with significant tree cover—cottonwood, poplar, or high-pollen ornamental species—benefit from more frequent attention.

For homes where screens stay in place year-round, annual cleaning at minimum prevents the gradual accumulation that reduces screen performance and shortens frame life.
bullets: Allergy symptoms worsen with windows open: screen pollen loading likely
Reduced airflow through open windows: mesh clogged enough to restrict passage
Visible pollen, mildew, or cottonwood fibres embedded in mesh
Post-winter assessment: moisture-driven growth typically present on year-round screens

sections.h2: DIY Screen Cleaning: What Works and What Doesn't
sections.body:
DIY screen cleaning is genuinely manageable for most homeowners with the right approach. The tools required are simple, and the technique is forgiving compared to glass cleaning. The common mistakes are in force and drying.

The effective DIY screen cleaning process: remove each screen and lay it flat on a clean surface (or lean it against a fence). Use a soft brush—a natural-bristle paintbrush or a dedicated screen cleaning brush—to dry-brush loose debris off both faces of the mesh before introducing water. This step removes cottonwood fibres and loose pollen before it can become muddy paste when wet.

After dry brushing, rinse the screen with a garden hose on a gentle setting—not a pressure washer or strong jet stream, which can distort the mesh or damage spline seals. Work with the flow of water, not against it, and cover both faces. For screens with mildew growth, a dilute solution of dish soap or mild cleaner can be applied with the soft brush after the initial rinse.

After washing, shake excess water and prop the screen to drain and air-dry completely before reinstalling. Reinstalling damp screens introduces moisture to the track and frame, contributing to the mould conditions you were trying to address.

The mistake most homeowners make is using a pressure washer at too-high a setting, which bows aluminum frames and distorts fine mesh. The second common mistake is reinstalling screens while still damp—this traps moisture behind the spline seal and promotes mould at the frame corners.

For screens with significant mildew, embedded cottonwood, or visible frame corrosion, DIY cleaning may produce limited results. Professional cleaning using appropriate solutions and proper rinsing technique is more thorough.

sections.h2: Professional Screen Cleaning vs. DIY: Where the Gap Is
sections.body:
Professional screen cleaning produces better results in specific situations—not because the task is inherently complex, but because professionals use appropriate solutions, have the equipment to handle volume efficiently, and apply technique developed through repetition.

For standard dusty or lightly-pollen-coated screens, thorough DIY cleaning produces results very close to professional quality. The difference is small for simple cases.

The gap widens for: screens with embedded cottonwood fibres in fine mesh (professional brushing technique and solution dwell time removes more material), screens with significant mildew growth (professional cleaners use mould-killing solutions and technique that ensures full treatment of frame corners), and screens with visible corrosion or staining on aluminum frames (professional cleaning can address these without using abrasives that damage mesh).

A practical advantage of professional screen cleaning is systematic organization. When a home has 15–20 screens removed, washed, and ready for reinstall, the risk of putting the wrong screen in the wrong window is significant. Wrong-size screens either don't seat properly or are forced into place, distorting the frame. Professional cleaners mark or organize screens by window position before removal and reinstall each screen in its correct location.

Cottonwood in the Shuswap is a specific professional advantage case. Cottonwood fibres are not easily removed by hose rinsing—they embed in mesh and interlock with neighbouring fibres. A stiff brush with appropriate solution, applied with enough contact force to dislodge the fibres without damaging mesh, removes more material than most homeowners achieve with a garden hose.
bullets: DIY works well for standard dust and light pollen accumulation
Professional cleaning outperforms DIY on embedded cottonwood fibres
Mildew treatment requires appropriate solutions and complete coverage of frame corners
Screen organization and correct reinstallation is a professional process advantage

sections.h2: Shuswap-Specific Challenges: Cottonwood and Lake Humidity
sections.body:
The Shuswap region presents screen-fouling conditions that are more severe than most Canadian communities face. Two regional factors dominate.

Cottonwood pollen season in the Salmon Arm area typically runs from late May through June, with intensity varying by year. Cottonwood releases voluminous fibrous material that is both visible and persistent. Unlike tree pollen, which is a fine powder that rinses away easily, cottonwood fibres are structural—they interlock with the mesh structure of fine-gauge screens and resist rinsing. A week of open windows during peak cottonwood can pack fine-mesh screens to the point of near-complete air blockage. The problem is worse with newer, finer-mesh screens (such as 20-mesh or finer insect screens) than with standard 18x16 fiberglass mesh.

Lake humidity from Shuswap Lake and associated waterways creates persistently elevated moisture conditions throughout the summer. Screens that hold moisture—especially at frame corners where the spline seal meets aluminum—develop mould reliably. The mould colony at a screen corner isn't typically visible from the ground; it's discovered when the screen is removed for cleaning. But it produces spores that enter the home through the screen mesh whenever the window is open and a breeze flows through.

Aluminum frames are subject to corrosion in high-humidity environments, particularly near the lake. Fine white corrosion deposits on aluminum frame surfaces indicate oxidation—cleaning can remove surface deposits, but significant corrosion signals a screen that should be replaced rather than repeatedly cleaned.

For Shuswap homeowners, the practical recommendation is: remove and inspect all screens in late spring (after cottonwood), clean thoroughly, inspect frames for corrosion, and reinstall only screens in good condition. Screens showing significant frame corrosion or mesh damage should be replaced.

sections.h2: Screen Inspection: What to Check During Cleaning
sections.body:
Screen cleaning is the natural opportunity to inspect each screen's condition. Assessments made during cleaning inform replacement decisions and prevent damaged screens from being reinstalled where they won't perform their function.

Mesh integrity: Look for tears, holes, or stretched sections. Even small holes defeat the insect-exclusion function of the screen. Pets are a common cause of mesh damage at lower windows; cottonwood debris and pressure washing can also cause damage over time.

Frame condition: Bent or bowed aluminum frames don't seal against the window opening properly. A minor bow can be pressed back into shape; significant deformation means the screen should be replaced. Corrosion—visible as white powder deposits on aluminum—is cosmetic at early stages and structural at advanced stages.

Spline condition: The rubber spline holds the mesh in the frame channel. Old or dried spline can pop out of the channel when the screen is handled, releasing the mesh. Spline replacement is a DIY repair (the material costs a few dollars and the tool costs about $10); it's worthwhile if the screen frame itself is in good condition.

Rubber seal condition: The foam or rubber sealing strip at the screen perimeter—where the screen contacts the window frame—should be intact and compressible. Deteriorated seals allow insects to enter around the screen perimeter.

Wagon Windows inspects each screen during removal and cleaning and communicates findings to the homeowner. If a screen needs spline replacement or has mesh damage, we note it. Replacement recommendations are based on condition, not routine replacement pressure—if a screen is in good shape, we say so.
bullets: Mesh: check for tears, holes, stretch marks from pets or pressure washing
Frame: check for bowing, corrosion, or deformation that prevents proper sealing
Spline: confirm it seats fully in the channel and isn't cracked or hardened
Edge seal: confirm compressible foam/rubber seal is intact around perimeter

faqs.q: How often should window screens be cleaned in the Shuswap?
faqs.a: Twice yearly is appropriate for most Shuswap-area homes: once in late spring after cottonwood season, once in fall. Lakefront properties and homes with heavy cottonwood tree coverage may benefit from additional attention during peak cottonwood in May–June. Screens that stay in place year-round should be inspected and cleaned at least annually.

faqs.q: Will a garden hose remove cottonwood from window screens?
faqs.a: Partially. A garden hose on a gentle setting removes loose cottonwood before it embeds deeply. Once cottonwood fibres have interlocked with the mesh structure, rinsing alone is insufficient. A soft brush with cleaning solution, worked carefully into the mesh from both sides, removes more embedded material than rinsing alone. Avoid high-pressure settings, which distort mesh and damage frames.

faqs.q: Can mould on window screen frames affect indoor air quality?
faqs.a: Yes. Mould colonies on screen frames—particularly at corners where the spline meets the frame—produce spores that are carried indoors on air currents whenever the window is open. For occupants with mould allergies or respiratory sensitivities, this can produce noticeable symptoms. Cleaning with an appropriate mould-killing solution and ensuring screens dry completely before reinstallation addresses the issue.

faqs.q: Does Wagon Windows clean and reinstall window screens?
faqs.a: Yes. Screen cleaning is available as part of our residential window service. We remove, clean, inspect, and reinstall each screen in its correct window position. Screens are organized by location before removal to prevent the common problem of reinstalling a screen in the wrong window. Ask about screen cleaning when you book—call (403) 837-5092.

cta_text: Cottonwood season takes a toll on Shuswap window screens. Book Wagon Windows for screen cleaning as part of your spring service. Call (403) 837-5092.

meta_title: Exterior vs Interior Window Cleaning: What's Included
meta_desc: What does exterior window cleaning include vs interior? Understand the difference in scope, tools, and when exterior-only makes sense for your home.
h1: Exterior vs Interior Window Cleaning: What Each Service Includes and When You Need Both
intro: Exterior window cleaning and full interior-exterior window cleaning sound like simple variations on the same service—but they're distinct processes requiring different tools, different access logistics, and different time investments. Understanding what each service includes helps you choose the right option for your home, your schedule, and your budget.

sections.h2: What Exterior Window Cleaning Includes
sections.body:
Exterior window cleaning addresses the outside face of every accessible window pane on the home. It's the more commonly booked service for a simple reason: the exterior faces are by far the dirtier surfaces. Weather, dust, pollen, bird activity, spider webs, and—in the Shuswap—mineral deposits from lake spray and irrigation all accumulate on exterior glass. Interior surfaces, by contrast, accumulate primarily household dust, occasional fingerprints, and condensation residue.

A thorough exterior cleaning includes: removal of any loose debris, cobwebs, and dry contaminants from the glass surface before solution is applied (dry brushing); application of professional cleaning solution via T-bar applicator; squeegee removal with the single-pass technique; edge detailing with a lint-free cloth; and a final wipe of the exterior windowsill and frame surround.

Screen removal before exterior cleaning and reinstallation after is standard—screens left in place block direct access to the glass and produce inferior results. When screens are removed as part of the service, they're typically shaken or brushed to remove loose debris before reinstallation. Full screen cleaning is a separate service from this light removal-and-reinstall step.

Exterior cleaning also addresses frame condition in a functional sense: the squeegee and sill wipe process removes surface debris from frames, though it doesn't include deep track cleaning (a separate service) or pressure washing of frames.

For most residential properties, exterior cleaning is the most impactful single service. The visual difference is dramatic—dirty exterior glass makes the whole home look neglected; clean exterior glass has an immediate curb appeal effect that interior cleaning alone cannot achieve.

sections.h2: What Interior Window Cleaning Adds
sections.body:
Interior window cleaning addresses the inside face of each window pane. When booked as a full service alongside exterior cleaning, the cleaner works both faces of each window in sequence—typically exterior first, then interior, so any light exterior residue doesn't contaminate the interior glass on the return pass.

The interior process is similar in technique—T-bar applicator, squeegee, edge detailing—but differs in access logistics and tool management. Interior cleaning requires moving furniture or objects near windows, working in occupied living spaces, and managing water more carefully to prevent floor or surface contact. Professional cleaners work with cloths to catch drips immediately and take care around soft furnishings, electronics, and any surfaces near the window.

Interior cleaning also addresses accumulated condensation residue and film that forms on interior glass over time. In kitchens and bathrooms, cooking vapour and steam contribute to a greasy film that doesn't exist on exterior glass. In living areas, dust mixed with outgassing from furnishings creates a subtle haze that's most visible in oblique light. These interior-specific deposits require the same squeegee technique as exterior cleaning but may need a slightly more concentrated solution or a second application for heavier films.

Interior cleaning typically adds 30–60 minutes to a full-home service, depending on window count and access complexity. It's priced in addition to exterior cleaning—ask about the combined price when booking.
bullets: Interior service adds access logistics: furniture movement, floor protection, drip management
Kitchen and bathroom interior glass develops grease and steam film not present on exteriors
Living area glass develops dust-and-outgassing haze visible in oblique light
Adds 30–60 minutes to a full-home service; priced in addition to exterior

sections.h2: When Exterior-Only Makes Sense
sections.body:
For many homeowners and many scheduling situations, exterior-only cleaning is the right choice—not a compromise, but a deliberate and sensible selection.

Exterior-only is appropriate when: interior windows are reasonably clean (regular household maintenance keeps interior glass in acceptable condition); the primary motivation is curb appeal or pre-event preparation; scheduling requires minimal home access or occupation time; or budget requires prioritizing the highest-impact service.

Because exterior glass accumulates far more contamination than interior glass, exterior cleaning produces proportionally more visual impact per dollar spent. A home with clean exterior glass and slightly dusty interior glass looks significantly cleaner than the reverse—the exterior face is what catches light from outside and what guests and passersby see.

Exterior-only is also appropriate for homeowners who prefer to clean interior windows themselves or who have pets or small children making coordinating interior access logistically complex. The service can be completed efficiently with no one home, while interior cleaning typically benefits from the homeowner being present to move objects near windows.

For pre-sale preparation, the decision depends on buyer demographic and showing conditions. In strong seller's markets, exterior-only usually covers the visual requirement. In competitive markets where buyers scrutinize thoroughly, full interior-exterior cleaning ensures no detail reads as neglected.

Wagon Windows offers exterior-only and full interior-exterior packages. We'll recommend what makes sense for your situation when you call—not upsell for the sake of it.

sections.h2: How the Two Services Differ in Execution
sections.body:
Beyond scope, exterior and interior window cleaning differ in the tools used, the cleaning solutions appropriate, and the care required during execution.

Exterior cleaning is physically active work—cleaners move around the building, use extension poles and ladders for upper floors, manage buckets and equipment on outdoor surfaces, and work in weather conditions (wind, direct sun, temperature variations). The work is efficient because access is relatively uncomplicated and drips and water contact with exterior surfaces are generally not a concern.

Interior cleaning requires more careful management. Solution application must account for walls, floors, and furnishings within splatter range. Squeegee pulls must be controlled to prevent water from running off the sill onto interior surfaces. Drop cloths or towels are positioned at the sill line to catch any water. Cleaners working indoors are also more careful about footwear, equipment placement, and communication with the homeowner about what needs to be moved.

The cleaning solution used may differ slightly. Interior glass that carries cooking film or condensation residue may benefit from a slightly higher surfactant concentration than standard exterior cleaning solution. Ammonia-based solutions are sometimes used for interior kitchen glass where grease film is present—but not on tinted or coated glass, where ammonia can cause discolouration.

Professionally, the two services are often performed by the same cleaner on the same visit, moving systematically through the home. For large homes with many windows, some companies use two-person teams—one cleaner working exterior, one interior—to reduce total visit time.

sections.h2: Which Service Is More Popular and Why
sections.body:
In the Shuswap residential market, exterior-only cleaning is the more commonly booked service, and the reasons are practical.

First, exterior glass is the dirtier surface by a significant margin. Shuswap-area homes face weather exposure, pollen, cottonwood, and mineral deposits from lake and irrigation water. Exterior glass can go from professionally clean to visibly dirty in a single week during peak cottonwood or following a rain event that leaves mineral deposits. Interior glass, by comparison, accumulates slowly.

Second, exterior cleaning requires less homeowner coordination. There's no need to be home, no need to move furniture, and no concern about cleaners in occupied living spaces. For busy homeowners or properties that are seasonally occupied (many Shuswap lake properties are), the ability to book exterior cleaning without being present is a meaningful convenience.

Third, exterior cleaning produces the visible impact that motivates the decision to book in the first place. Homeowners notice dirty exterior glass from the driveway. The improvement from a professional exterior clean is immediately visible and satisfying in a way that interior cleaning—which addresses subtler accumulation—is not.

Full interior-exterior cleaning is more common for pre-sale preparation, post-construction cleaning, spring deep-cleaning projects, and clients who want comprehensive service on a seasonal schedule. These homeowners value the complete result over the convenience of exterior-only access.

Wagon Windows books both services regularly across Salmon Arm and the Shuswap. Call (403) 837-5092 to discuss what's right for your home and timeline. Residential exterior starts at $169.

faqs.q: What's the difference between exterior and interior window cleaning?
faqs.a: Exterior window cleaning addresses the outside face of every accessible pane—the dirtiest surface, exposed to weather, pollen, and mineral deposits. Interior cleaning addresses the inside face, which accumulates household dust, condensation residue, and cooking film more slowly. Full service covers both; exterior-only is the more commonly booked option and produces most of the visible impact.

faqs.q: Do I need to be home for exterior window cleaning?
faqs.a: Generally no. Exterior cleaning is conducted entirely outside the home and doesn't require interior access. Cleaners work around the building perimeter and can complete the service without the homeowner present. Interior cleaning does benefit from the homeowner being home to facilitate object movement near windows, though this can be arranged in advance.

faqs.q: How much more does interior window cleaning cost compared to exterior-only?
faqs.a: Interior cleaning adds 30–50% to a typical residential exterior cleaning price, depending on home size and access complexity. The additional cost reflects the added time, care, and logistics of working indoors. Wagon Windows prices the combined service transparently—call (403) 837-5092 for a quote that covers both.

faqs.a: Yes. We offer exterior-only and full interior-exterior residential cleaning. Exterior cleaning starts at $169. Full interior-exterior cleaning is quoted based on home size and window count. We recommend the right service for your situation—we don't push interior if exterior alone serves your needs.

meta_title: Professional Window Cleaning Process: Step by Step
meta_desc: See exactly how Wagon Windows cleans your home's windows—from walkthrough to final inspection. Know what a thorough job looks like and what corners cheap services cut.
h1: The Professional Window Cleaning Process: A Step-by-Step Guide to How Wagon Windows Works
intro: Professional window cleaning looks simple from the outside—a cleaner shows up, does their work, and leaves behind spotless glass. What's less visible is the systematic process that makes that outcome consistent. This is the full Wagon Windows process, step by step—every stage, why it matters, and what separates thorough service from the shortcuts that cheaper operators take.

sections.h2: Step 1: Walkthrough and Pre-Service Assessment
sections.body:
Before any cleaning begins, Wagon Windows conducts a walkthrough of the property with the homeowner or, on unoccupied properties, a systematic independent assessment. This step is non-negotiable and sets the entire service up for success.

The walkthrough accomplishes several things. First, it identifies access challenges: is the area under each ladder placement stable? Are there irrigation heads, decorative stones, or plantings that require care during approach? Are there second-storey windows with unusual setbacks or architectural features?

Second, it identifies special conditions: visible mineral deposits requiring treatment? Post-construction residue? Windows with specialty coatings that preclude standard chemical protocols? Any broken seals in double-pane units that will fog after cleaning (addressing homeowner expectation: a broken seal fogs regardless of how the glass is cleaned—this isn't a cleaning failure)?

Third, it confirms scope: which windows are included in the service (all accessible panes, or specific inclusions/exclusions)? Are screens to be cleaned as part of the service or just removed and reinstalled? Are tracks included?

Fourth, it notes any pre-existing glass damage that the homeowner should be aware of before cleaning begins—chips, cracks, delaminating film—so that there's no ambiguity about when damage occurred.

This walkthrough is documented. Anything noteworthy is noted in the service agreement. Homeowners are informed of any findings that affect scope, pricing, or expected outcome before work begins. This transparency protects both parties and ensures expectations are aligned.
bullets: Access assessment: ladder placement stability, obstacles, architectural complexities
Special condition identification: mineral deposits, post-construction residue, coated glass
Scope confirmation: which windows, screens, tracks, and services are included
Pre-existing damage notation: chips, cracks, broken seals noted before cleaning begins

sections.h2: Step 2: Screen Removal and Organization
sections.body:
Once the walkthrough is complete, screens are removed systematically. This sounds simple—it isn't, at scale.

A home with 20 windows has 20 screens, each of which fits one specific window. Screens that appear similar in size often have subtle differences—a millimetre difference in height or width, a screen with a different mesh gauge installed in a particular window, or a screen with a repair that the homeowner knows about and wants kept in a specific location. Installing the wrong screen in the wrong window means either a screen that doesn't seat properly (creating an insect-entry gap) or a screen forced into place (bowing the frame).

Wagon Windows removes screens in a documented sequence, marking or organizing them by window position before moving on. The organization system ensures every screen goes back to its original location. This seems like overhead—it is, but it prevents a problem that generates complaints in operations that don't do it.

During removal, screens are inspected for damage: mesh tears, spline condition, frame bowing, corrosion. Any findings are communicated to the homeowner. Screens with significant damage are flagged for replacement discussion rather than silently reinstalled.

Removed screens are placed in a safe, organized location away from the working area—not leaning against the wall where a ladder could knock them, not in the path of water runoff from cleaning.

sections.h2: Step 3: Dry Debris Removal and Pre-Rinse
sections.body:
Before any cleaning solution touches the glass, dry debris is removed. This step is skipped by under-equipped operators, and its absence is felt in the result.

Dry debris on exterior glass includes: spider webs and insect carcasses at frame corners, loose cottonwood fibres, pollen dust, bird droppings that haven't been moistened, and any dry soil from splash-back at lower panes. When cleaning solution is applied to glass that hasn't been dry-cleared, this debris becomes wet debris—it smears across the glass during the T-bar application stroke, redistributing rather than removing it.

A soft brush (a natural-bristle brush or professional glass-detailing brush) clears webs and insect material from frame corners and the glass surface. A dry microfibre pass removes loose pollen and dust. On panes with significant cottonwood accumulation, a more deliberate dry brush pass removes as much fibre as possible before moisture is introduced.

Bird droppings deserve specific attention. Fresh or recently-moistened droppings are acidic—they actively etch glass if allowed to dwell and are also corrosive to certain frame coatings. Dried droppings require pre-softening with water before they can be removed without abrasion risk. The pre-rinse step—a light water mist or wet cloth applied to dried droppings—allows them to soften while the dry brush work continues on other windows.

For homes with significant organic debris (under trees, near bird nesting areas), the dry-debris step takes proportionally longer than on a cleaner home. It's time well spent: rushing past it produces inferior glass results.

sections.h2: Steps 4–6: Solution Application, Squeegee, and Edge Detailing
sections.body:
With dry debris removed and any softening done, the active cleaning sequence begins. This three-step sequence—solution, squeegee, detail—is repeated on every pane.

Solution application via T-bar: the sleeve, saturated with cleaning solution at the appropriate concentration for local water hardness, is applied to the glass in full-coverage strokes. The solution is spread evenly and allowed a brief dwell—typically 15–30 seconds on standard glass, longer on panes with mineral deposit concerns. During dwell time, the solution breaks down surface tension, penetrates mineral deposits, and loosens organic debris.

Squeegeeing: the rubber squeegee is pulled across the glass using the single-pass technique appropriate for the pane size and orientation. For small or divided-light windows, straight horizontal pulls are most controlled. For large architectural windows, the fan technique or straight vertical pulls are used depending on orientation. Each stroke overlaps the previous by approximately half an inch to capture any water that crept past the blade edge. The blade is checked and flipped if any chattering or inconsistency in the pull is noticed.

Edge detailing: after each pane is squeegeed, a lint-free cloth is used to remove the water bead at the frame perimeter. The cloth is pressed to the edge with a knuckle or folded finger, drawing down and across each side of the frame. The detailing cloth is refolded to a clean section after every two or three panes to ensure a dry surface is always in contact with the glass. A wet detailing cloth smears rather than absorbs—it's one of the most common causes of frame-edge residue that homeowners notice in oblique light.
bullets: Solution at calibrated concentration for Shuswap water hardness—never standard dilution without water assessment
Dwell time extended on mineral deposit situations—not rushed to the next step
Squeegee rubber checked and flipped if chatter develops—blade quality is maintained during the job
Detailing cloth refolded to dry section every 2–3 panes—wet cloth smears, dry cloth absorbs

sections.h2: Steps 7–10: Interior Cleaning, Screen Reinstall, and Final Inspection
sections.body:
For full interior-exterior services, the interior cleaning follows the exterior sequence using the same T-bar, squeegee, and detailing approach—with the additional care required for working in occupied living spaces. Drop cloths at sill lines, immediate drip management, and communication about anything moved near windows are standard interior protocol.

Screen reinstallation follows interior cleaning. Screens are returned to their documented positions, seated fully in the track channel, and tested for proper engagement—pressed at corners to confirm the frame seats without bowing or gap. Any screen that doesn't seat properly is flagged immediately rather than forced.

The final inspection is a walk-around assessment of every cleaned window. Each pane is viewed from multiple angles in available light—including oblique viewing that reveals any residual haze, streaking, or edge water lines not visible from straight-on. Any pane that doesn't meet standard is re-cleaned on the spot.

Photographic documentation is taken of any pre-existing damage noted during the initial walkthrough, and of the completed work where appropriate. This documentation protects both the cleaner (clear record of pre-existing conditions) and the homeowner (confirmation of completion and condition).

The service agreement records what was completed, any findings communicated during the job, and any follow-up recommendations (screens needing replacement, tracks needing separate service, broken seals that should be addressed). This paper trail means there's no ambiguity about what was done and what was communicated.

Wagon Windows doesn't leave a job until the inspection is complete and the homeowner (where present) is satisfied. Call (403) 837-5092 or visit wagonwindow.com to schedule. Residential exterior from $169.

faqs.q: How long does professional window cleaning take for a typical home?
faqs.a: A standard 2,000–2,500 sq ft Salmon Arm home with 20–25 windows takes a Wagon Windows crew approximately two to three hours for exterior-only service. Full interior-exterior cleaning adds 30–60 minutes depending on access complexity and number of windows. Homes with significant mineral deposit treatment or track cleaning included take longer.

faqs.q: What happens if a window is missed or doesn't meet quality standards?
faqs.a: Wagon Windows conducts a post-cleaning inspection before leaving the property. Any pane that doesn't meet standard is re-cleaned immediately. If you identify an issue after we leave, call us—we'll return and correct it. Service quality is our reputation, and we don't consider a job complete unless the result meets the standard we'd accept on our own home.

faqs.q: Do I need to be home during the window cleaning service?
faqs.a: For exterior-only cleaning, no—the service is conducted entirely outside and can be completed while you're away. For interior cleaning, being home to open windows and identify any objects near windows that need moving is helpful, though we can work around most situations with advance communication. Leaving a gate unlocked and confirming payment method before departure is sufficient for most exterior-only bookings.

faqs.q: What does Wagon Windows do if they find a broken window seal during cleaning?
faqs.a: We note it during the initial walkthrough or during cleaning and communicate it to the homeowner. A broken seal in a double-pane unit causes fogging between the panes regardless of how the glass is cleaned—it's not a cleaning failure, and we're transparent about that distinction. We don't clean inside a broken seal. We'll recommend the homeowner contact a window supplier about replacement, and we document the finding in the service record.
